meta.purpose: Define terminology boundaries, common misunderstandings, and self-checks for test-driven development judgment.
meta.load_condition: Always loaded when this domain is active.

standard_terms.definition: The TDD cycle: write a failing test (RED), make it pass (GREEN), then clean up the implementation (REFACTOR).
A thin end-to-end cut through all integration layers that delivers and tests one observable behavior.
A test that specifies expected observable behavior (what), not implementation details (how).
A test coupled to implementation details that fails when implementation changes even if behavior is preserved.
A structural point in the code where behavior can be observed or replaced without modifying production code.
The simplest code that makes the current test pass, intentionally lacking generalization until a subsequent test demands it.
The first vertical slice that proves the architecture works end-to-end, establishing the pattern for subsequent slices.

banned_terms.why: This phrase reduces TDD to a timing convention. TDD is not about when tests are written; it's about driving design through behavior specification.
banned_terms.replace_with: specify the expected behavior with a failing test, then implement

banned_terms.why: Coverage measures which lines are executed, not which behaviors are verified. High coverage with brittle tests provides false confidence.
banned_terms.replace_with: behavioral coverage — which observable behaviors are verified by tests that can fail

banned_terms.why: Pursuing 100% coverage incentivizes writing tests that cannot fail, which provides no verification value.
banned_terms.replace_with: every behavior has a test that can fail

banned_terms.term: integration tests are better than unit tests
banned_terms.why: This false dichotomy ignores that both serve different purposes. Unit tests verify behavior contracts; integration tests verify wiring. Both are needed.
banned_terms.replace_with: behavioral tests at the appropriate level of isolation

misunderstandings.wrong: TDD means writing all the tests before writing any implementation code.
misunderstandings.correct: TDD is an incremental cycle: one failing test, then minimal implementation to pass it, then refactor. Each cycle is complete before the next begins.
misunderstandings.key_distinction: All tests first ≠ One test at a time. Writing all tests upfront is waterfall testing, not TDD.
misunderstandings.why: Writing all tests first loses the design feedback loop — the primary value of TDD. Each test should influence the design of the next.

misunderstandings.wrong: Hardcoding return values in the GREEN phase is cheating or bad practice.
misunderstandings.correct: Hardcoding is the correct TDD response when only one test case exists. It signals that generalization is premature. The next test will drive the generalization.
misunderstandings.key_distinction: Hardcoding during GREEN ≠ Hardcoding in production. Hardcoding during GREEN is driven by the current test; generalization is driven by the next test.
misunderstandings.why: Premature generalization during GREEN produces over-engineering. Let each test demand exactly the complexity it requires.

misunderstandings.wrong: TDD is too slow. It's faster to write the code first and add tests later.
misunderstandings.correct: TDD is slower per feature but faster per project. The feedback loop catches design problems and bugs immediately, avoiding expensive late debugging.
misunderstandings.key_distinction: Speed per feature ≠ Speed per project. TDD's incremental cost pays off in reduced debugging and rework.
misunderstandings.why: Code-first approaches accumulate technical debt that manifests as increasing debugging time. TDD keeps debugging time constant.

misunderstandings.wrong: If I mock all dependencies, my unit tests are isolated and therefore good.
misunderstandings.correct: Over-mocking creates tests coupled to implementation details — the definition of brittle tests. Mocks should be used at seams, not to isolate every dependency.
misunderstandings.key_distinction: Isolation ≠ Over-mocking. Proper isolation uses seams to verify behavior at boundaries. Over-mocking replaces real dependencies with test-specific behavior, coupling tests to internals.
misunderstandings.why: Over-mocked test suites break when implementation changes even though behavior is preserved, eroding trust and eventually being deleted.

self_check: Did I write the failing test before the implementation?
Does this test specify behavior (what) or implementation (how)?
Can this test fail? What condition would make it fail?
Am I refactoring while any test is RED?
Am I adding complexity that no current test requires?
Would this test still pass if the implementation changed but behavior didn't?
